title: Figure code
text:
This chapter documents how the figures are generated. The code blocks are not evaluated during website rendering; they show the plotting logic. Final figures are called from saved PDF files in figures/raw_distributions/, figures/model_effects/, figures/experiment_interactions/, and figures/temperature_interactions/.




Show figure setup code
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(readr)
library(ggeffects)
library(glmmTMB)

dat <- readRDS("data/prepared_nestlings.rds")
selected_models <- read_csv("models/selected_models.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

trait_labels <- c(
  D2_MASS = "Body mass day 2",
  D8_MASS = "Body mass day 8",
  D12_MASS = "Body mass day 12",
  D12_RTARS = "Tarsus length day 12",
  Early.g = "Early growth",
  Late.g = "Late growth",
  Growth = "Whole-period growth",
  D12_SMI = "Scaled mass index day 12",
  D12_SURV = "Survival to day 12"
)





These figures show the raw response distributions before model interpretation. For continuous responses, the box centre line is the median, the box spans the interquartile range, and pale points are individual nestlings.


Show raw-distribution code
plot_raw_distribution <- function(response) {
  plot_data <- dat |>
    filter(!is.na(.data[[response]]))
  y_label <- trait_labels[[response]] %||% response

  if (response == "D2_MASS") {
    p <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = EXP.INC, y = .data[[response]], fill = EXP.INC)) +
      geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.72, outlier.alpha = 0.25) +
      geom_jitter(width = 0.12, alpha = 0.22, size = 1) +
      labs(x = "Incubation treatment", y = y_label)
  } else {
    p <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = EXP.INC, y = .data[[response]], fill = EXP.INC)) +
      geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.72, outlier.alpha = 0.25) +
      geom_jitter(width = 0.12, alpha = 0.22, size = 1) +
      facet_wrap(~ EXP.NEST) +
      labs(x = "Incubation treatment", y = y_label, fill = "Incubation")
  }

  p <- p +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("#476F67", "#C98C1E", "#8FAEA3", "#6E93A2")) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom")

  ggsave(file.path("figures", "raw_distributions", paste0("raw_", response, ".pdf")),
         p, width = 7.2, height = 4.8)
}





These figures use model predictions as the main signal. Dark points and lines show fitted means from the selected mixed model, vertical intervals are 95% confidence intervals, and pale jittered points show original observations in the background.


Show continuous model-effect code
plot_continuous_model_effect <- function(response) {
  fit <- readRDS(file.path("models", paste0("best_model_", response, ".rds")))
  y_label <- trait_labels[[response]] %||% response

  if (response == "D2_MASS") {
    raw_data <- dat |>
      filter(!is.na(.data[[response]]), !is.na(EXP.INC))
    pred <- ggpredict(fit, terms = "EXP.INC")

    p <- ggplot() +
      geom_jitter(
        data = raw_data,
        aes(x = EXP.INC, y = .data[[response]]),
        width = 0.12,
        alpha = 0.16,
        size = 1,
        color = "grey35"
      ) +
      geom_pointrange(
        data = pred,
        aes(x = x, y = predicted, ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high),
        size = 0.62,
        linewidth = 0.55,
        color = "#1F3A3D"
      ) +
      labs(x = "Incubation treatment", y = paste("Predicted", y_label))
  } else {
    raw_data <- dat |>
      filter(!is.na(.data[[response]]), !is.na(EXP.INC), !is.na(EXP.NEST))
    pred <- ggpredict(fit, terms = c("EXP.INC", "EXP.NEST"))

    p <- ggplot() +
      geom_jitter(
        data = raw_data,
        aes(x = EXP.INC, y = .data[[response]]),
        width = 0.13,
        alpha = 0.13,
        size = 0.9,
        color = "grey35"
      ) +
      geom_line(
        data = pred,
        aes(x = x, y = predicted, color = group, group = group),
        linewidth = 0.85
      ) +
      geom_pointrange(
        data = pred,
        aes(x = x, y = predicted, ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high, color = group, group = group),
        size = 0.58,
        linewidth = 0.5,
        position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
      ) +
      facet_wrap(~ EXP.NEST) +
      labs(x = "Incubation treatment", y = paste("Predicted", y_label), color = "Nestling treatment")
  }

  p <- p +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("#476F67", "#C98C1E", "#6E93A2", "#8FAEA3")) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom")

  ggsave(file.path("figures", "model_effects", paste0("model_effect_", response, ".pdf")),
         p, width = 7.2, height = 4.8)
}





The interaction figures use the same visual rule as the primary figures: model predictions are the main signal and raw data are shown as pale background points when the plot remains readable.


Show interaction-figure code pattern
# Experimental interaction:
fit <- readRDS("models/experiment_interaction/experiment_interaction_models_D12_MASS.rds")
fit <- fit$interaction_gaussian
pred <- ggpredict(fit, terms = c("EXP.INC", "EXP.NEST"))

ggplot() +
  geom_jitter(
    data = dat |> filter(!is.na(D12_MASS)),
    aes(x = EXP.INC, y = D12_MASS),
    width = 0.13,
    alpha = 0.14,
    size = 0.9,
    color = "grey35"
  ) +
  geom_line(aes(x = x, y = predicted, color = group, group = group), data = pred) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(x = x, y = predicted, ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high, color = group), data = pred)

# External-temperature interaction:
fit <- readRDS("models/sensitivity_temperature_interaction/temperature_interaction_models_D12_MASS.rds")
fit <- fit$interaction_gaussian
pred <- ggpredict(fit, terms = c("incubation_temperature_sc [all]", "nestling_temperature_sc [-1,0,1]"))

ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data = dat |> filter(!is.na(D12_MASS)),
    aes(x = incubation_temperature_sc, y = D12_MASS),
    alpha = 0.14,
    size = 0.8,
    color = "grey35"
  ) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(x = x, ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high, fill = group), data = pred, alpha = 0.16) +
  geom_line(aes(x = x, y = predicted, color = group), data = pred)





The final figures are saved as PDF files and can be opened directly from the website.

title: Experimental interaction models
text:
This page tests whether the effect of nestling-stage warming depends on incubation-stage warming. Biologically, this asks whether conditions experienced before hatching modify later responses to the nestling-period treatment.
The interaction model is:


response ~ EXP.INC * EXP.NEST + SEX + BS_sc + (1|F_RING) + (1|YEAR)


Day-2 mass is not included here because it occurs before most of the nestling-stage treatment.

title: Distributional sensitivity analysis
text:
For continuous nestling traits, the primary models use Gaussian errors. Student-t models are fitted as robustness checks because AIC can favour heavier-tailed distributions even when the observed trait distributions do not strongly depart from normality.
AIC is therefore reported as descriptive model-comparison information, not as the sole criterion for choosing the biological model. The main interpretation remains based on the Gaussian mixed models; Student-t results are used to check whether treatment-effect directions and support are robust to the distributional assumption.

title: Distributional sensitivity analysis
text:
Effects that show the same direction in Gaussian and Student-t models are treated as directionally robust. The asterisk indicates whether the effect also has statistical support (p < 0.05). Effects that change direction, or have support only under one distributional assumption, are interpreted cautiously as distribution-sensitive results.
When the estimated effect changed direction between Gaussian and Student-t models, this indicates that the result was sensitive to distributional assumptions. We therefore do not interpret this effect as robust evidence for a biological relationship.

title: Data preparation
text:
This chapter documents how the nestling-level dataset was prepared before model fitting. Each step below gives the purpose of the operation, a foldable code block showing the exact procedure, and the resulting dataset state.


The nestling-level workflow starts from one raw workbook, CF_exp_all_2026.xlsx, using the sheet CF_exp.nestlings_analysis. This raw sheet is not treated as the final analysis file because repeated females are removed in a later preparation step. Only variables used later in the analysis are retained at import.


Show source-data code
raw_nestlings <- read_excel(
  "CF_exp_all_2026.xlsx",
  sheet = "CF_exp.nestlings_analysis",
  na = c("NA", "", "na", "NaN")
)

analysis_vars <- c(
  "F_RING", "LD", "INC", "CS", "BS", "HD", "SEX", "YEAR",
  "EXP.INC", "EXP.NEST",
  "D2_MASS", "D8_MASS", "D12_MASS", "D12_RTARS", "Early.g", "Late.g", "Growth",
  "LOGMEAN_INCUBATION", "LOGMEAN_NESTLING", "D12_SURV", "GROUP", "BOX", "TEMP.BOX",
  "TEMP.BOX.ENV", "BLOCK"
)

nestlings_selected <- raw_nestlings |>
  dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(analysis_vars))


Result: the source sheet contains 934 nestling records before filtering.



Continuous measurement variables are converted to numeric values. Treatment, identity, year, sex, survival, box, and block variables are converted to factors.


Show variable-type code
numeric_vars <- c(
  "LD", "INC", "CS", "BS", "HD", "Growth", "Early.g", "Late.g",
  "D2_MASS", "D8_MASS", "D12_RTARS", "D12_MASS",
  "LOGMEAN_INCUBATION", "LOGMEAN_NESTLING", "TEMP.BOX", "TEMP.BOX.ENV"
)

factor_vars <- c(
  "F_RING", "SEX", "YEAR", "EXP.INC", "EXP.NEST", "D12_SURV", "GROUP", "BOX", "BLOCK"
)

nestlings_typed <- nestlings_selected |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(dplyr::any_of(c("SEX", "EXP.INC", "EXP.NEST", "GROUP")),
                  ~ stringr::str_squish(as.character(.x))),
    dplyr::across(dplyr::any_of(numeric_vars), ~ suppressWarnings(as.numeric(.x))),
    dplyr::across(dplyr::any_of(factor_vars), as.factor)
  )


Result: variables have the classes required by the mixed models.



The analysis is restricted to nestlings sexed as F or M. Records with missing or other sex codes are removed before model fitting.


Show sex-filter code
nestlings_typed <- nestlings_typed |>
  dplyr::filter(SEX %in% c("F", "M")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(SEX = droplevels(SEX))


Result: 885 sexed nestling records are retained.



The analysis keeps one experimental brood per female. Broods are identified by F_RING, YEAR, BOX, and LD. If a female appears in more than one brood, the first brood after sorting by YEAR, LD, and BOX is retained, and later broods from the same female are removed.


Show repeated-female code
brood_order <- nestlings_typed |>
  dplyr::distinct(F_RING, YEAR, BOX, LD, .keep_all = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::arrange(F_RING, YEAR, LD, BOX) |>
  dplyr::group_by(F_RING) |>
  dplyr::mutate(female_brood_order = dplyr::row_number()) |>
  dplyr::ungroup()

repeated_females <- brood_order |>
  dplyr::count(F_RING, name = "n_broods") |>
  dplyr::filter(n_broods > 1)

retained_broods <- brood_order |>
  dplyr::filter(female_brood_order == 1) |>
  dplyr::transmute(F_RING, YEAR, BOX, LD, retained_brood = TRUE)

prepared_nestlings <- nestlings_typed |>
  dplyr::inner_join(retained_broods, by = c("F_RING", "YEAR", "BOX", "LD")) |>
  dplyr::select(-retained_brood) |>
  droplevels()


Result: one female had repeated brood records. Removing the later brood reduced the dataset from 885 to 883 nestling records, representing 169 retained broods. The dataset after this step is the corrected nestling-level analysis dataset used by the models.



Brood size, hatching date, and measured temperatures are standardised before modelling. The original temperature columns LOGMEAN_INCUBATION and LOGMEAN_NESTLING are used to create clearer scaled-variable names; the final analysis file keeps the scaled variables used by the models.


Show standardisation code
prepared_nestlings <- prepared_nestlings |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    HD_sc = as.numeric(scale(HD)),
    BS_sc = as.numeric(scale(BS)),
    incubation_temperature_sc = as.numeric(scale(LOGMEAN_INCUBATION)),
    nestling_temperature_sc = as.numeric(scale(LOGMEAN_NESTLING))
  )


Result: the model covariates are BS_sc, HD_sc, incubation_temperature_sc, and nestling_temperature_sc.



Day-12 scaled mass index is calculated from day-12 body mass and day-12 tarsus length:
[ SMI = M_i ()^b ]
where M_i is day-12 mass, L_i is day-12 tarsus length, L_0 is the mean tarsus length, and b is the standard-major-axis slope approximated as the ratio of standard deviations of log mass and log tarsus.


Show SMI code
smi_base <- prepared_nestlings |>
  tidyr::drop_na(D12_MASS, D12_RTARS) |>
  dplyr::filter(D12_MASS > 0, D12_RTARS > 0) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    log_mass_n = log(D12_MASS),
    log_tars_n = log(D12_RTARS)
  )

b_sma_chicks <- sd(smi_base$log_mass_n, na.rm = TRUE) / sd(smi_base$log_tars_n, na.rm = TRUE)
L0_chicks <- mean(smi_base$D12_RTARS, na.rm = TRUE)

prepared_nestlings <- prepared_nestlings |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    D12_SMI = dplyr::if_else(
      !is.na(D12_MASS) & !is.na(D12_RTARS) & D12_MASS > 0 & D12_RTARS > 0,
      D12_MASS * (L0_chicks / D12_RTARS)^b_sma_chicks,
      NA_real_
    )
  )


Result: day-12 scaled mass index is available for 679 nestlings.



The retained data are summarised by the four treatment combinations. Both the number of retained nests and the number of retained nestling records are shown.


Show treatment-count code
treatment_counts <- prepared_nestlings |>
  dplyr::count(EXP.INC, EXP.NEST, GROUP, name = "nestling_records") |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    prepared_nestlings |>
      dplyr::distinct(F_RING, YEAR, BOX, LD, EXP.INC, EXP.NEST, GROUP) |>
      dplyr::count(EXP.INC, EXP.NEST, GROUP, name = "nests"),
    by = c("EXP.INC", "EXP.NEST", "GROUP")
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(EXP.INC, EXP.NEST, GROUP, nests, nestling_records)

After the preparation checks and treatment counts are created, the file used for modelling is reduced to the columns needed for the analyses. This prevents the shared analysis file from carrying unused raw or intermediate variables.


Show final-column selection code
analysis_columns <- c(
  "F_RING", "YEAR", "SEX",
  "EXP.INC", "EXP.NEST", "GROUP",
  "D2_MASS", "D8_MASS", "D12_MASS", "D12_RTARS",
  "Early.g", "Late.g", "Growth", "D12_SMI", "D12_SURV",
  "BS_sc", "incubation_temperature_sc", "nestling_temperature_sc"
)

analysis_nestlings <- prepared_nestlings |>
  dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(analysis_columns))

saveRDS(analysis_nestlings, "data/prepared_nestlings.rds")
readr::write_csv(analysis_nestlings, "data/prepared_nestlings.csv")


Result: the final analysis dataset contains 883 nestling records and 18 columns.

title: Project overview
text:
This website documents a reproducible analysis workflow for the nest-heating experiment in collared flycatchers (Ficedula albicollis) on Gotland.





Conceptual overview of the collared flycatcher nest-heating experiment on Gotland, showing paired adults, nestlings, and contrasting thermal environments.




This project tests how experimentally increased nest temperature affects reproductive performance and offspring development in a wild population of collared flycatchers (Ficedula albicollis). Using a fully factorial 2 x 2 design, we independently warmed nests during incubation and during the nestling period. This created four treatment combinations and allowed us to separate thermal effects acting before hatching from those acting after hatching.
We analyse reproductive performance at two key stages: hatchability, defined as the probability that eggs hatch, and post-hatching survival, defined as the probability that hatched young survive to fledging. In addition, we examine offspring development by analysing nestling growth rate, body size, and body mass. These traits allow us to test whether thermal conditions affect not only survival, but also the developmental trajectory and condition of surviving young.
The analyses separate two related questions. The primary results test whether the experimental warming treatments affect offspring traits and survival. Additional model pages test whether the two experimental stages interact, and whether measured external temperature during incubation and the nestling period is associated with the same responses.


The study was conducted on collared flycatchers (Ficedula albicollis) breeding in nest boxes on Gotland. The dataset currently covers four breeding seasons, 2022-2025. The experiment manipulated the thermal environment of nest boxes during incubation and/or the nestling period, allowing comparison of heated and control nests.



The experiment includes two treatment variables:

EXP.INC: incubation-period treatment, coded as heated or control.
EXP.NEST: nestling-period treatment, coded as heated or control.

The combined treatment group is stored as GROUP, with four combinations: CONCON, CONEXP, EXPCON, and EXPEXP. Nests were paired into blocks according to clutch/egg number and hatching date, and this paired structure is represented by BLOCK in the source dataset.





Experimental design of the stage-specific nest warming experiment.







The website documents the full analytical workflow, from data preparation and treatment coding to response-variable construction, model fitting, diagnostics, sensitivity analyses, prediction plots, and biological interpretation.
The workflow is organised around five steps:

data import from the raw workbook,
creation of the analysis dataset after removing later broods from repeated females,
fitting primary experiment models with glmmTMB,
fitting separate interaction models for experimental treatments and measured external temperature,
generating model summaries, diagnostics, and publication figures.




The original database is kept in the repository as CF_exp_all_2026.xlsx. The analyses shown on this website use the prepared nestling-level analysis dataset data/prepared_nestlings.rds, with a CSV copy available as data/prepared_nestlings.csv. The prepared file was created from the original database after filtering sexed nestlings, removing later broods from repeated females, deriving response variables, and retaining only columns used in the analyses.

Standardised measured incubation-period temperature, derived from LOGMEAN_INCUBATION.


nestling_temperature_sc
Standardised measured nestling-period temperature, derived from LOGMEAN_NESTLING.

title: Model strategy
text:
The analyses are organised around two biological questions that are kept separate throughout the website.
First, we ask whether the experimental warming treatment itself affects nestling performance. These are the primary results. They include treatment predictors, sex, brood size, female identity, and year, but they do not include measured ambient temperature covariates.
Second, we ask whether measured external temperature is associated with the same responses. These models are shown separately because they address a different question: not whether the experimental manipulation worked, but whether natural variation in outside temperature during incubation and the nestling period is related to offspring phenotype or survival.


The dependent variables are:

day-2 body mass (D2_MASS),
day-8 body mass (D8_MASS),
day-12 body mass (D12_MASS),
early growth (Early.g),
late growth (Late.g),
whole-period growth (Growth),
day-12 scaled mass index (D12_SMI),
day-12 tarsus length (D12_RTARS),
day-12 survival (D12_SURV).

Day-2 body mass is treated separately because it occurs before most of the nestling-period treatment. All later responses can include both incubation-period and nestling-period predictors.



For continuous nestling traits, Gaussian mixed models are used as the primary models. This choice is based on interpretability and on the fact that the response distributions do not show strong departures from normality. Student-t models are still fitted because AIC often favours heavier-tailed error distributions, but they are treated as robustness checks rather than as an automatic replacement for the Gaussian models.
AIC values are reported descriptively in the distributional sensitivity analysis. Effects that are consistent between Gaussian and Student-t models are interpreted as more robust. Effects that appear only under one distributional assumption are treated cautiously.
For day-12 survival, the primary model uses a binomial error distribution.



For day-2 body mass, the primary model tests incubation-stage treatment only:


D2_MASS ~ EXP.INC + SEX + BS_sc + (1|F_RING) + (1|YEAR)


For later continuous traits, the primary experiment model is:


response ~ EXP.INC + EXP.NEST + SEX + BS_sc + (1|F_RING) + (1|YEAR)


For day-12 survival, the same fixed-effect structure is fitted with a binomial error distribution:


D12_SURV ~ EXP.INC + EXP.NEST + SEX + BS_sc + (1|F_RING) + (1|YEAR)





A separate page tests whether incubation-stage warming changes the response to nestling-stage warming:


response ~ EXP.INC * EXP.NEST + SEX + BS_sc + (1|F_RING) + (1|YEAR)


These models address carry-over effects of the experimental treatment. They are presented separately from the primary additive experiment models so that the main treatment effects and interaction question remain easy to read.



Measured external temperature is analysed on a separate page using an interaction between incubation-period and nestling-period temperature:


response ~ EXP.INC + EXP.NEST + incubation_temperature_sc * nestling_temperature_sc + SEX + BS_sc + (1|F_RING) + (1|YEAR)


These models ask whether natural variation in external temperature during one stage modifies the association between temperature in the other stage and the response. They are not the primary experimental test; they are used to separate the biological effect of the manipulation from associations with background thermal conditions.

title: Ambient-temperature interaction models
text:
This page addresses a separate question from the primary experiment results: whether measured ambient temperature is associated with offspring traits and survival.
The aim is not to replace the experimental treatment models, but to show whether natural variation in ambient temperature during incubation and the nestling period is related to the same responses. These models include the treatment predictors and then add the interaction between measured incubation-period and nestling-period ambient temperature:


response ~ EXP.INC + EXP.NEST + incubation_temperature_sc * nestling_temperature_sc + SEX + BS_sc + (1|F_RING) + (1|YEAR)


Day-2 mass is not included because it occurs before most of the nestling-period treatment.


Before interpreting the ambient-temperature interaction models, we check whether the two measured temperature predictors are strongly correlated and whether the fitted models show problematic collinearity.


The Pearson correlation between `incubation_temperature_sc` and `nestling_temperature_sc` is `r = -0.062` based on `n = 840` complete records. This indicates no strong linear correlation between the two measured ambient-temperature variables.

The largest VIF in the ambient-temperature interaction models is `4.29`. The highest values occur for the interaction term, which is expected in interaction models. Values remain below the common cautionary threshold of 5, so there is no evidence of severe collinearity.
